coredata.dc:title: Modulating the fluorescence properties of carbon dots by varying the stoichiometric ratios of citric acid and urea during the synthesis
coredata.dc:creator: MS, Jeesna
Nambiar, Abhishek
A, Sobha
coredata.dc:description: Varying the ratios of citric acid and urea during the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) provides a strategy to modulate their fluorescence properties. The impact of different precursor ratios on the resulting CDs' size, surface chemistry, and structure was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis and confirmed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to study the influence of precursor ratios on the types and densities of surface functional groups. The optical properties of the CDs were examined using a UV–visible absorption spectrometer, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). Smaller CDs generally exhibit higher energy emissions, resulting in bluer light, while larger CDs tend to emit lower energy light, appearing redder. By adjusting the precursor ratios, it is possible to induce shifts in fluorescence emission, leading to CDs with different fluorescence colours. Furthermore, the antimicrobial activity of CDs against bacterial pathogens was assessed, and their potential for promoting plant growth was explored by coating seeds with concentrated and diluted CDs solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence emission
Growth promoters
Antimicrobial Activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of samples for Antibacterial study
Optical characterization: Fluorescence property of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their gratitude to DST-FIST and CPE for the research facilities provided at Providence Women's College, Calicut, India. The authors acknowledge the KINFRA a techno-industrial park in Calicut for access to the bacterial study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) samples under sunlight (b) under UV light (sample 1 to sample 5 as 1–5).
(a): XRD of CDs, (b): Particle size distribution from XRD data, (c): TEM image of sample 1.
FTIR spectrum of CDs (sample 1 to sample 5).
(a-e): Emission spectrum of CDs (sample 1 to sample 5). (f). The excitation spectrum of CDs at 360 nm.
Antibacterial studies in a) Escherichia coli, b) Streptococcus pyogenes, c) Klebsiella pneumonia d) Staphylococcus aureus, e) Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
Normal seeds (Left) and CDs coated seeds (Right) a) Vigna unguiculata b) Pisum sativum.
(a-e): Effect of CDs on the taproot growth in seeds for uncoated, single, and double-coated (left to right in each figure) different seeds.
Absorption wavelengths and corresponding optical bandgap of CDs.

head.ce:title: Modulating the fluorescence properties of carbon dots by varying the stoichiometric ratios of citric acid and urea during the synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Providence Women’s College, Malaparamba, Kozhikode 673009 Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Providence Women’s College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Providence Women’s College, Malaparamba, Kozhikode −673009, Kerala

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram 695019, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram 695019, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Varying the ratios of citric acid and urea during the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) provides a strategy to modulate their fluorescence properties. The impact of different precursor ratios on the resulting CDs' size, surface chemistry, and structure was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis and confirmed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to study the influence of precursor ratios on the types and densities of surface functional groups. The optical properties of the CDs were examined using a UV–visible absorption spectrometer, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL). Smaller CDs generally exhibit higher energy emissions, resulting in bluer light, while larger CDs tend to emit lower energy light, appearing redder. By adjusting the precursor ratios, it is possible to induce shifts in fluorescence emission, leading to CDs with different fluorescence colours. Furthermore, the antimicrobial activity of CDs against bacterial pathogens was assessed, and their potential for promoting plant growth was explored by coating seeds with concentrated and diluted CDs solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a captivating class of carbon-based nanomaterials with a wide range of applications, owing to their unique properties. Their exceptional optical properties, biocompatibility, and low toxicity have garnered significant attention [1]. In recent years, various synthesis methods, including hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, solvothermal, and citric acid-based approaches, have been developed to precisely control the size, surface functionalization, and optical properties of CDs [2–6].

One of the most remarkable features of CDs is their photoluminescence properties, characterized by bright and tunable fluorescence emission [7]. This attribute has led to their utilization in diverse fields such as bioimaging, biosensing, and optoelectronics [8]. CDs have found applications as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging, enabling the detection of neurotransmitters, heavy metals, and biomolecules [9–12]. Through surface passivation and doping, CDs' emission wavelength and colour can be finely tuned, facilitating the development of multicolour fluorescent CDs [13,14].

Moreover, CDs possess tunable chemical and photoelectric properties, making them excellent candidates for antibacterial theragnostic applications [15–17]. They exhibit great chemical stability, high water solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [18–20] which can be utilized effectively in the agriculture field [21–23]. Recent studies have shown that nitrogen-containing carbon promotes the creation of active oxygen species, which is correlated with their electron-donating properties [24,25]. The antimicrobial capacity of CDs has been a current research area, and many have reported targeting Gram-positive and Gram-negative microbes through electrostatic interaction between the anionic microbial membrane and cationic residues on the surface of the CDs [15,18,26,27]. This opens up the possibility of using CDs as important substitutes for conventional antibiotic drugs in antibacterial testing. The unique structural and chemical properties of CDs allow for a wide range of applications. Typically, CDs consist of sp2 carbon cores with abundant surface functional groups, allowing for chemical modifications and surface engineering [28–30].

In this work, we synthesized CDs using an oil bath method in a laboratory setting and modulated their luminescence properties by adjusting the stoichiometric ratio of urea and citric acid. The luminescent properties of the CDs make them valuable for various applications, and ongoing research explores their uses in agriculture. CDs, known for their non-cytotoxic nature [31], have been employed as growth promoters in the agricultural field. Coating seeds with a concentrated CDs solution has demonstrated a two to three-times higher growth rate compared to uncoated seeds, along with enhanced resistance to bacterial or fungal infections. Furthermore, investigations into the efficiency of CDs as replacement photosensitizers and their effectiveness in promoting growth and reducing the reliance on toxic pesticides and fertilizers in agriculture can be conducted. These versatile properties and applications of CDs hold great potential for advancements in numerous fields, and further research and exploration are expected to unveil even more exciting possibilities.

Different ratios of citric acid (x = 1.6,1.4,1.2,1,0.8) g and urea (2-x) g are weighed out using a chemical balance, mixed evenly with 25 g of distilled water, and placed in five boiling tubes labelled as samples 1–5. In the oil bath method, 200 ml of palm oil with a boiling temperature estimated at 300 °C (572 F) is carefully placed within a beaker. The oil is preheated to a temperature slightly above the desired experimental value. After reaching the desired temperature, the heat output is adjusted to maintain it. A boiling tube with the mixture is placed in a beaker filled with palm oil. The entire setup is positioned atop a low-flame burner, maintaining a steady temperature of 140 °C for approximately 1.5 h. To ensure temperature consistency, a thermometer/ temperature probe is immersed directly into the oil bath, providing real-time monitoring of any temperature fluctuations. The boiling temperature of palm oil used for oil bath purposes is estimated at 3000 C (or 572 F). The mixture in the boiling tube is kept inside the beaker of palm oil and the setup was kept on a low flame burner for approximately 1.5 h at a constant temperature of 1400 C. The solid residue after boiling was transferred into a China dish and was dried for four hours at 900 C. These samples were dissolved in distilled water and constantly stirred using a magnetic stirrer for a homogenous solution. The solution was subjected to centrifugation in a Rotek laboratory centrifuge operating at 4000 rpm, generating a relative centrifugal force of approximately 1745 times the force of gravity (g). This centrifugal force facilitated the separation of larger-sized particles, which were subsequently isolated and utilized for the investigation of their optical properties. The whole experimental procedure is shown in Fig. 1.

The structural characterization of CDs was conducted using the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. XRD patterns for all samples were obtained at room temperature using a Rigaku-Mini Flex 600 diffractometer. The diffractometer employed Cu-kα (1.5406 Å) radiation filtered through nickel. Parameters such as an excitation voltage of 40 kV, a step size of 0.020, and a scanning speed of 50/min were set. Additionally, the current was maintained at 15 mA during the measurements. To determine the crystallite size of the CDs, XRD data was analyzed and further confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Surface functional and chemical properties were studied using FTIR. Optical properties, including the absorption spectrum of the CDs, were investigated using a UV–visible double-beam spectrophotometer. The optical bandgap of the CDs was evaluated based on the absorption wavelengths obtained from the spectrophotometer measurements. The fluorescence properties of the samples were assessed using a Perkin Elmer (LS55) instrument in the visible range.

By employing these characterization techniques, a comprehensive analysis of the CDs' structural, optical, and fluorescence properties was obtained, providing valuable insights into their properties and potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples for Antibacterial study
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial efficacy of the synthesized fluorescence carbon dots (CDs- sample1) against Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Streptococcus pyogenes was evaluated using the agar diffusion method. To prepare the nutrient agar media, a mixture of peptone (0.5 g), yeast extract (0.3 g), and agar (3 g) was dissolved in 100 ml of distilled water in a conical flask, resulting in a total volume of 100 ml. Both the Petri dishes and the nutrient agar media were sterilized at 120 °C for 20 min to ensure aseptic conditions and prevent contamination.

After cooling the prepared nutrient agar media to room temperature, it was poured into sterile Petri dishes. The bacterial inoculums were freshly prepared and evenly swabbed onto the surface of the nutrient agar medium using a sterilized cotton swab, ensuring uniform distribution of the bacteria across the plate. Two wells were made in the agar media: one for loading the CDs and the other for adding a reference antibiotic (Chloramphenicol) to determine the inhibition zone.

A stock solution of CDs was prepared by mixing them with sterile distilled water, resulting in a concentration of 0.03 mg/ml. This CDs solution was loaded into one of the wells, while the antibiotic (Chloramphenicol) was added to the other well as a reference for measuring the inhibition zone. The Petri dishes were then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C to allow bacterial growth. After incubation, the formed inhibition zone (in mm) on the surface of the nutrient agar medium in the Petri dish was observed and measured. This measurement provided insights into the antibacterial activity of the synthesized fluorescence CDs against the tested bacterial strains.

By conducting these experiments, we aimed to assess the potential of the synthesized CDs as antibacterial agents, providing valuable data on their effectiveness in inhibiting the growth of Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Streptococcus pyogenes.

In the agricultural domain, we utilized synthesized carbon dots (CDs- Sample 1) for a specific application. The seeds were subjected to a coating process, whereby they were coated with pure distilled water, diluted CDs (0.1 mg/ml) and concentrated CDs (0.2 mg/ml). After coating, the seeds were allowed to dry for a period of 3 h. To assess the impact of the CDs on seed growth, three sets of seeds were prepared: normal untreated seeds (distilled water dipped sample), seeds coated with diluted CDs, and seeds coated with concentrated CDs. All three sets of seeds were cultivated in the same environment under identical conditions. After a growth period of 20 days, the growth rates of the three sets of seeds were compared and analysed. This study aimed to determine the effect of CDs, whether diluted or concentrated, on seed growth and development. By conducting this experiment, we sought to explore the potential of CDs in enhancing seed growth in the agricultural sector, providing insights into their impact on plant development.

The oil bath method was employed to facilitate a polymerization reaction between citric acid and urea, resulting in the formation of a polymer-like material. Subsequently, through carbonization and pyrolysis, Carbon Dots (CDs) were generated. The citric acid (C6H8O7), an organic acid, and urea (CH4N20), an organic compound containing nitrogen sources, played crucial roles in this process. Urea, specifically, contains two NH2 groups connected by a carbonyl (CO) functional group.

Upon exposure to UV light, samples 1 and 2 exhibited vibrant blue emissions, while sample 3 displayed a light blue colour (cyan). Samples 4 and 5, on the other hand, emitted a green light. The presence of amino groups as auxochromes enhanced the optical absorption of the CDs, resulting in a deeper absorption colour. Notably, urea acted as a surface passivating agent, contributing to the increased luminescence of the prepared CDs. Under sunlight, the CDs displayed a brownish-red colour, whereas, under UV light, they exhibited fluorescence, as illustrated in Fig. 2(a) and (b) respectively. This characterization of the CDs provides valuable insights into their optical properties and behaviour under different lighting conditions.

The XRD analysis revealed the crystal structure, as depicted in Fig. 3(a). The XRD data indicated a distinct broad peak around 270 with a lattice spacing (d) of approximately 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) planes. The presence of this broad peak in samples 1 and 2 suggested a disordered pattern of the CDs, which is consistent with previous reports on CDs [32].

To determine the crystalline size, Scherrer's Eq. (1) was utilized:
(1)
D =
K λ
B cos θ

In the equation, D represents the diameter of the CDs, K is a constant based on the crystal shape (0.89), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray (0.15406 nm), B represents the full-width half maximum (FWHM) of the characteristic peak of the CDs, and θ represents the scattering angle. The resulting stabilized particle sizes are presented in Fig. 3(b), with sample 1 exhibiting the smallest particle size of around 1.2 nm, and sample 5 displaying the largest particle size of around 6.1 nm.

Furthermore, a TEM image of one of the synthesized CDs (sample 1) was captured and shown in Fig. 3(c). This TEM image confirmed the accuracy of the particle size measured using Scherrer's equation, aligning with an average particle size in the range of 1–1.3 nm obtained from the TEM image analysis. Additionally, the TEM image confirmed the spherical nature of the prepared CDs. These findings from XRD analysis, particle size determination, and TEM imaging provide valuable insights into the crystal structure, size, and morphology of the synthesized CDs.

The FTIR spectra of the prepared CDs are presented in Fig. 4. The FTIR spectra of CDs with higher urea content show broad and strong bands above 3000 cm−1. Urea is a compound that contains nitrogen atoms, and when it is added to citric acid, it can undergo various reactions that lead to the incorporation of nitrogen in the resulting material. One possible reaction is the condensation of citric acid and urea, which can form amide or imide linkages. These linkages can introduce nitrogen atoms into the structure and result in the formation of functional groups such as amides or imides. The presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups can lead to changes in the vibrational modes of the bonds, including the stretching vibrations of C-H bonds. The increase in peak intensity above 3000 cm−1 may be due to the newly formed functional groups containing nitrogen and additional C-H bonds. As the content of urea increases, the presence of sharp bands due to the CO (carbonyl) stretching vibrations between 1500 and 2000 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum can be attributed to the formation of new carbonyl-containing functional groups. Urea contains a carbonyl group (-CO) itself, and when it is added to citric acid, it can participate in reactions that result in the formation of additional carbonyl groups. These reactions may involve condensation, esterification, or other chemical transformations.

The presence of urea can react with the carboxylic acid groups in citric acid, resulting in the formation of amide or imide linkages. These linkages contain carbonyl groups and can contribute to the appearance of sharp bands in the 1500–2000 cm−1 region of the FTIR spectrum. When urea is present in higher concentrations, it can undergo various reactions with citric acid or other components in the system. These reactions may introduce new chemical bonds or alter the bonding environment, leading to the appearance of new peaks in the FTIR spectrum. The region between 1000 and 1500 cm−1 is commonly associated with the stretching vibrations of C-C and C-N bonds, as well as vibrations involving aromatic rings and other functional groups. The presence of new small peaks in this region suggests the formation of new chemical species containing these bonds or modifications in the existing functional groups. The urea content does not have a noticeable impact on the intensity, position, or shape of the = CH bond peaks in the region between 1000 and 500 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum, it suggests that the = CH bonds are relatively unaffected by changes in the urea concentration.

The absorption spectrum of the synthesized CDs is presented in Fig. 5. It is evident that, with the exception of sample 5, the absorption wavelengths of all the samples are situated in the ultraviolet (UV) region, with a tail extending towards the UV–visible region. In the case of sample 5, an absorption wavelength of 404.92 nm is observed, which can be attributed to electron transitions of the surface functional groups of the CDs. This indicates that the surface chemical moieties play a role in the absorption in the UV–visible region, and the presence of surface hydroxy groups leads to strong and symmetric absorption peaks [33,34]. The surface hydroxy groups also act as passivating agents, reducing the presence of traps on the surface.

In contrast, samples 1–4 exhibit a lower number of surface hydroxy groups, resulting in incomplete passivation compared to sample 5. Consequently, the redshift in the absorption spectrum is less pronounced in these samples. As the urea content in the samples increases, there is an observable increase in the wavelength absorbance of the CDs. This indicates a significant redshift in the absorption spectrum due to enhanced surface passivation achieved through the presence of a higher number of surface hydroxy groups.

Overall, the absorption spectrum analysis provides valuable insights into the absorption properties of the synthesized CDs, highlighting the influence of surface functional groups and their role in determining the absorption wavelengths and passivation effects inconsistent with the FTIR results.

The optical band gap of the Carbon Dots was determined from the UV–visible absorption spectrum using the Tauc relation [35], as illustrated in Fig. 6. By extrapolating the tangent drawn, the intercept on the (h*υ)-axis provides the value of the optical energy gap (Eg).

The Tauc relation (2) is given by:
(2)
α h ϑ
1 / n
= C
h ϑ
− Eg

Here, Eg represents the bandgap energy, α is the optical absorption coefficient, υ represents the frequency of light photons, C is a constant, and n depends on the type of transition (n = 1/2 for direct allowed transition).

From Fig. 5, it is evident that the optical bandgap gradually decreases from sample 1 to sample 5. Sample 1 exhibits the highest bandgap, and it gradually blue-shifts towards sample 5. This trend is further supported by the data presented in Table 1, which displays the absorption wavelength and optical bandgap of the synthesized CDs. It is apparent that as the size of the CDs increases, the optical bandgap decreases. These results align with the crystalline size measurements obtained from the XRD analysis.

The analysis of the optical bandgap provides valuable insights into the energy levels and electronic properties of the synthesized CDs, demonstrating the relationship between their size, absorption properties, and bandgap energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization: Fluorescence property of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs exhibit captivating fluorescence emission, making them widely applicable. Fig. 6(a-e) demonstrates the Stokes PL emission of CDs. By adjusting the particle size, the PL emission of CDs can be tuned since their sizes are smaller than the exciton Bohr radius. CDs with diameters ranging from 1.2 to 2.31 nm display PL emissions across the UV range, with the smallest CDs at 1.2 nm emitting at 440 nm and GQDs with a diameter of 2.31 nm emitting at 560 nm. Increasing the diameter of GQDs from 1.2 to 6.2 nm leads to PL emissions spanning the visible region (440–600 nm) due to the quantum confinement effect. Notably, Kim et al. have reported size- or shape-dependent PL emission in nanometer-sized GQDs, where electronic transitions can be tuned by altering size or shape. The emission spectrum of prepared CDs aligns with the absorption spectra, as evident in Fig. 6(f) where the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum demonstrate consistency with the absorption spectrum.

Fig. 7(a-e) illustrates the observed antibacterial mechanism of Carbon Dots (CDs) of sample 1 on different bacteria. The antibacterial study was conducted following the aforementioned procedures to evaluate the impact of CDs on both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. CDs were found to possess antibacterial properties, effectively inhibiting bacterial growth. This antibacterial behaviour can be attributed to the presence of various functional groups within the CDs, which can interfere with cellular enzyme functions and impede cellular proliferation [31].

The CDs contain nitrogen elements that carry positive charges, enabling their interaction with negatively charged microbes. Moreover, the Carbon Dots can penetrate the cell membrane, leading to the demise of the microorganisms [31]. Notably, the CDs exhibit higher inhibition towards Klebsiella pneumonia, a Gram-negative bacterium. This observation opens avenues for developing carbon nanomaterials with exceptional and targeted antibacterial activity, which could find applications in clinical settings.

Table 2 provides details of the zone of inhibition resulting from the antibacterial activity of CDs against bacterial pathogens. These findings demonstrate the potential of Carbon Dots as effective agents for combating bacterial infections. Fig. 8.

Fig. 9(a &b) presents a comparison between normal seeds and seeds coated with CDs of sample 1. The coating of Vigna unguiculata and Pisum sativum seeds with CDs resulted in noticeable changes compared to the normal seeds. After a growth period of 20 days, the plants that were grown from the coated seeds and the normal seeds were analysed. The measurement of tap root growth is depicted in Fig. 10(a-e).

In a study conducted by Ping Zhao's group in 2021, it was reported that CNQDs (Carbon Nanodots) coated on green beans exhibited non-toxic properties and promoted good germination and root growth [36]. Similarly, in this study, a linear relationship can be observed between plant growth and the coating of seeds with concentrated and diluted CDs solutions. The plants derived from CDs-coated seeds demonstrated a significantly higher growth rate in taproots, exhibiting abundant fibrous roots compared to the uncoated seeds.

Moreover, it was observed that the uncoated seed plants, such as Vigna unguiculata, showed signs of fungal or bacterial infections, while the plants coated with CDs remained free from such infections. This suggests that the antibacterial properties of CDs may have prevented fungal/bacterial growth by permeating the plant cells. As the synthesized CDs exhibited non-cytotoxic characteristics and acted as growth promoters in agriculture, they hold great potential for future applications in the field.

In conclusion, this study successfully synthesized and characterized CDs through the polymerization reaction between citric acid and urea. The use of the oil bath method facilitated the formation of polymer-like material, which was then carbonized and pyrolyzed to generate the desired CDs. Citric acid and urea played crucial roles in this process, with urea acting as a surface passivating agent that enhanced the luminescence of the CDs.

The optical characterization revealed the unique properties of the synthesized CDs under different lighting conditions. The absorption spectrum analysis demonstrated the influence of surface functional groups on the absorption wavelengths, with a redshift observed as the urea content increased. The optical bandgap of the CDs decreased with increasing size, as confirmed by XRD analysis, which provided insights into their crystal structure and size. The photoluminescence emission spectra showed tunability of fluorescence properties based on the size of the conjugated pi domains.

The antibacterial activity of the CDs was evaluated, showing inhibitory effects on both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. This antibacterial behaviour was attributed to the presence of functional groups that interfere with bacterial enzyme functions. Notably, the CDs exhibited higher inhibition against Klebsiella pneumonia, suggesting potential targeted antibacterial applications in clinical settings.

In the agricultural field, the coating of seeds with CDs demonstrated positive effects. Plants derived from CD-coated seeds exhibited higher growth rates in taproots with abundant fibrous roots compared to uncoated seeds. Moreover, the coated seeds showed resistance to fungal or bacterial infections. The antibacterial properties of the CDs likely played a role in preventing such infections by permeating plant cells.

Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the synthesis, characterization, and potential applications of Carbon Dots. The optical properties, antibacterial activity, and agricultural benefits demonstrated by the synthesized CDs open doors for further exploration and utilization in various fields, including medicine, electronics, and agriculture.

Conception and design of the study: Dr. Sobha A. acquisition of data: Jeesna M S and Abhishek Nambiar. analysis and/or interpretation of data: Dr. Sobha A, Abhishek Nambiar, and Ms. Jeesna MS. Drafting the manuscript: Jeesna M S. Revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content: Abhishek Nambiar.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to DST-FIST and CPE for the research facilities provided at Providence Women’s College, Calicut, India. The authors acknowledge the KINFRA a techno-industrial park in Calicut for access to the bacterial study.
